content:
Notes and reflections from three discussions at Google I/O 2026 and KotlinConf 2026 on AI, software engineering, and the future of developer work.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Over the last few weeks, I watched three discussion-heavy sessions from Google I/O 2026 and KotlinConf 2026.
Unlike the usual conference talks that focus on new frameworks, language features, or product announcements, these sessions discussed something much more important:
How AI is changing the craft of software engineering itself.
The speakers came from different backgrounds and organizations, yet they all seemed to arrive at the same conclusion:
The future of software engineering is not about writing more code. It’s about understanding systems, making better decisions, and working effectively with AI.
As I went through these sessions, several common themes kept appearing. Here are the ideas that stood out the most.

Writing Code Was Never the Hard Part
One of the strongest messages across all three talks was surprisingly simple:
Writing code was never the hard part.
For years, many of us associated software engineering with our ability to write code quickly, memorize syntax, or master frameworks. But engineering has always been much more than programming.
A definition shared during one of the sessions described engineering as:
“Programming integrated over time.”
Anyone can build a prototype. AI can now generate one in seconds.
The real challenge begins when software needs to survive changing business requirements, scale, security reviews, production incidents, maintenance, and years of evolution.
This is where engineering starts.
The discussions repeatedly emphasized that critical thinking, system design, and trade-off analysis are becoming more valuable than the act of typing code.
Security versus performance.
Flexibility versus simplicity.
Speed versus maintainability.
These are decisions AI cannot fully make because they depend on business context and human judgment.
Jake Wharton reinforced a related idea:
Code is read far more than it is written.
The challenge is not producing code. The challenge is ensuring future engineers can understand and safely modify it as requirements evolve.

Senior Engineers Are Being Redefined
Historically, seniority often meant being able to write code that others couldn’t.
Today, that definition is changing.
One of the speakers described modern seniority as:
The ability to understand code others can’t.
That “others” now includes AI.
As AI becomes capable of generating increasingly large portions of code, the value of an engineer shifts from code production to code comprehension.
Senior engineers are becoming responsible for:

• Breaking problems into smaller components

• Performing trade-off analysis

• Validating AI-generated solutions

• Understanding complex systems

• Maintaining architectural consistency
The future senior engineer may spend less time writing code and more time ensuring the right code is being written.

AI Raised the Floor, Not the Ceiling
The rise of AI has dramatically lowered the barrier to building software.
People are already creating prototypes through what many now call “vibe coding” — describing what they want and allowing AI to generate an implementation.
This is a remarkable shift.
Non-developers can now build functional applications that would have required engineering teams only a few years ago.
But the speakers repeatedly highlighted an important distinction:
There is a massive gap between one-shotting a solution and battle-hardening it for production.
Generating a working feature is easier than ever.
Building secure, reliable, maintainable, observable software remains difficult.
AI has raised the floor.
It has not removed the ceiling.

The Risk Nobody Talks About: Cognitive Debt
One of the most interesting concepts discussed was something called cognitive debt.
Technical debt accumulates inside code.
Cognitive debt accumulates inside engineers.
When developers increasingly rely on AI to solve problems, generate implementations, and make decisions, they risk losing their own understanding of how systems work.
Over time, problem-solving abilities can erode because the thinking is being outsourced.
An even bigger risk is what the speakers called cognitive surrender.
This happens when developers blindly accept AI output without questioning it.
Without validation, critical thinking, or understanding, teams can slowly create what was described as a “house of cards” codebase — one that appears functional but lacks deep understanding from the people maintaining it.
The lesson wasn’t to avoid AI.
The lesson was to remain intellectually engaged.
One practical recommendation was adopting a habit of treating AI as an adversarial mentor.
After completing a task, ask:
“What did I miss? What isn’t right? Don’t be nice.”
Instead of treating AI as an unquestionable authority, use it as a critic that challenges your assumptions.

From Programming to Intent-Driven Engineering
Another recurring theme was that developers are moving away from being programmers and becoming intent-driven engineers.
Rather than focusing on language syntax, IDE shortcuts, or framework-specific details, engineers increasingly describe desired outcomes and architectural constraints.
The implementation details can then be delegated to AI.
Several experts even suggested “de-skilling” on syntax.
Not because programming languages no longer matter, but because memorizing every detail of every language is becoming less valuable.
Many experienced engineers now regularly work across multiple languages each week, using AI to bridge syntax gaps while focusing on the problem itself.
The emphasis is shifting from:
“How do I write this?”
to
“What exactly should be built?”
That is a fundamentally different skill.

The Rise of Agentic Engineering
One of the most fascinating ideas discussed was the emergence of agentic engineering.
Developers are increasingly working with multiple AI agents running tasks in parallel.
These agents can investigate bugs, generate tests, explore solutions, create documentation, and handle isolated engineering workstreams.
One speaker compared this experience to:
Doubling your team size with junior engineers.
But this introduces a new challenge.
Just as human engineers require context, AI agents require context too.
The sessions repeatedly highlighted the growing importance of high-quality documentation, architectural records, and clear design decisions.
The better your documentation, the more effective both humans and AI become.

Software Engineering Is Approaching a Tipping Point
The most thought-provoking session introduced the concept of Software Ecology.
Software Ecology is the study of the entire socio-technical system that produces software — the combination of people, tools, processes, architecture, culture, and technology.
The argument was simple:
AI will allow us to generate dramatically more code.
Potentially 10x.
Potentially 100x.
But the systems surrounding software development were never designed for that scale.
More code means:

• More reviews

• More testing

• More builds

• More dependencies

• More architectural complexity
As codebases grow, some challenges grow disproportionately.
Testing can become exponentially more expensive.
Compile times become longer.
Version control systems experience greater pressure.
Review processes become bottlenecks.
The problem is no longer generating code.
The problem is understanding and validating it.
This is where the concept of “shared fate” becomes important.
In large software ecosystems, every component affects every other component.
A small change in one area can ripple across thousands or millions of lines of code.
As AI accelerates software creation, understanding these interconnected systems becomes increasingly important.

AI Is an Amplifier, Not a Miracle
Perhaps the most important takeaway from all three sessions was this:
AI is a magnitude, not a direction.
AI amplifies whatever already exists within an engineering organization.
If a team has:

• Strong architecture

• Good documentation

• Healthy engineering culture

• Effective testing practices
AI amplifies those strengths.
If a team has:

• Poor documentation

• Weak engineering fundamentals

• Fragile architecture

• Unclear ownership
AI amplifies those weaknesses too.
The technology itself does not automatically improve engineering.
It accelerates whatever system it is introduced into.

Looking Ahead
My biggest takeaway from these sessions is that software engineering is not disappearing — it is evolving.
The industry often focuses on how quickly AI can generate code, but the more interesting question is what remains uniquely human.
The answer seems remarkably consistent across Google I/O and KotlinConf:

• Understanding business problems

• Framing the right questions

• Making trade-offs

• Designing architectures

• Maintaining long-term systems

• Exercising critical thinking
As codebases continue growing beyond what any individual can fully memorize, AI will likely become essential not only for generating code but also for helping us reason about increasingly complex systems.
The future engineer may write less code than previous generations.
But their responsibility will be greater than ever.
Because the real challenge was never writing code.
It was understanding the system behind it.
